data.title: Puig Brands: Market Turmoil Creates Exceptionally Attractive Entry Point
data.description:
Introduction

Puig Brands (OTCPK:PUIGF) (OTCPK:PUGBY) may not be a well-known name, but some of its products certainly are. The company owns several strong brands in the fragrance, makeup and skincare segment. The brand names Rabanne, Jean-Paul Gaultier and Carolina Herrera may ring a bell, as those represent the core brands owned by Puig. The Spanish company completed its IPO last year and has been on a pretty robust growth trajectory. The share price was recently hit pretty hard by the turmoil on the financial markets and, of course, its exposure to US tariffs. But this could very well create a buy opportunity for investors with a long-term vision.

Puig Brands is trading on the Madrid Stock Exchange with PUIG as its ticker symbol. The average daily volume is just over 500,000 shares per day, making it the most liquid listing to trade in the company’s shares. I will use the Euro as the base currency throughout this article.

Unfortunately, the website contains download-only links, but you can find all the relevant information here.

2024 was a strong year for the Spanish company

Puig Brands reported a total revenue of just under 4.8B EUR in 2023, an increase of 11.3% compared to the 2023 revenue. The COGS increased by a similar percentage, which also means the gross profit increased by a low single digit percentage to just under 3.59B EUR.

In fact, Puig’s margins remained pretty consistent as, although its distribution expenses increased by just 1%, other elements in the cost structure increased at a faster pace, including the 15%-plus increase in advertising and promotion, while the SG&A expenses increased by approximately 9.5%.

That also is the main reason why the operating profit increased by just 9.5% compared to the double-digit revenue increase. And on top of that, Puig Brands also recorded almost 147M EUR in "other operational expenses," which represents an 11-fold increase compared to the preceding year. Looking at the footnotes (shown below), the majority of those expenses are non-recurring in nature with almost 18M EUR spent on transaction expenses and 120M EUR was spent on IPO related items. This was however a non-cash item as it was mainly related to the extraordinary awards to incentivize the employees right before the IPO.

Those non-recurring items are the main reason why Puig Brands saw its reported operating profit decrease in 2024. Thanks to some non-recurring finance items, the pre-tax income as well as the net profit increased, and Puig reported a net income of almost 543M EUR, of which 530.6M EUR was attributable to its shareholders. This represented an EPS of 0.98 EUR per share.

In my original thesis, after the company completed its IPO, I had a closer look at Puig’s cash flows as well. The company reported an operating cash flow of 698.5M EUR before taking changes in the working capital position into account. We should, however, also deduct the almost 80M EUR in lease payments while the company also had to deal with approximately 40-45M EUR in cash interest expenses.

This results in an adjusted operating cash flow of approximately 575M EUR. And that’s plenty of cash flow to cover the 191M EUR in capital expenditures and the 265M EUR in acquisitions.

The free cash flow result was approximately 391M EUR or 72 eurocents per share. That sounds quite low as it’s also about 25% lower than the reported net income, but keep in mind the combination of capex and lease payments came in at 270M EUR compared to the 210M EUR in depreciation and amortization expenses.

Puig expects to grow again this year

Before diving into the 2025 guidance and the implications, it’s perhaps worth emphasizing the guidance that was provided before the recent turmoil on the financial markets started.

The 2025 outlook calls for a like-for-like revenue increase of 6-8% while the adjusted EBITDA margin should increase at a similar pace as what we witnessed in 2024. Last year, the adjusted EBITDA margin increased by 20 bps to 20.2%, which implies this year’s adjusted EBITDA margin will be approximately 20.4%. This is based on the management’s internal estimates when it came to the anticipated US tariffs, so the margin expectations may perhaps have to be revisited as I doubt the company was expecting the severity of the currently proposed tariff structure in the US. The Americas represented about 36% of the 2024 revenue, but this of course also includes about a dozen other countries and not just the US. Puig has not provided how much of its revenue it actually generates in the US but assuming it’s about half of that revenue, and it will be subject to a 20% tariff of which half can be passed on to the consumer, the total impact of the tariffs would be less than 100M EUR In the first year. Just to emphasize: This is not the company’s official guidance, but just my personal expectation.

Using an adjusted EBITDA margin of 20% (which takes a 20 bps decrease into consideration vs. last year, compared to an anticipated margin increase of 20 bps) and assuming a 6% revenue increase to 5.08B EUR, the anticipated adjusted EBITDA margin should be approximately 1.02B EUR.

Assuming 220M EUR in depreciation and amortization expenses, the EBIT would be around 800M EUR. I'm anticipating 40M EUR in net interest expenses which implies a pre-tax profit of 760M EUR. Applying a 25% tax rate (the standard corporate tax rate in Spain) should result in a net profit of 570M EUR, of which approximately 560M EUR should be attributable to the shareholders of Puig. That results in an EPS of 1.03 EUR per share.

If the revenue increased by 7% and in case Puig is indeed able to increase its adjusted EBITDA margin to 20.4%, the implied EBITDA in this scenario would be approximately 30M EUR higher, leading to a 4-5 cent per share increase in the EPS.

Investment thesis

Don’t expect any aggressive growth from Puig Brands. But if the company can continue to grow its revenue at a mid to high single-digit percentage per year (as was outlined in its IPO documentation) while keeping the EBITDA margin stable (I’m not even counting on the small gradual margin increases), the EBITDA will continue to grow, toward 1.2B EUR in 2027.

At the end of 2024, Puig had 883M EUR in cash and approximately 1.65B EUR in gross debt for a net financial debt of around 770M EUR. That being said, the balance sheet also contains approximately 1.09B EUR in put/call liabilities related to acquisitions.

Puig’s modus operandi is to initially acquire a smaller stake in a company and to gradually increase that stake, offering the sellers a put/call structure depending on how the business performs. While it’s not financial debt per se, it's something to keep an eye on as it still represents approximately 1x EBITDA.

At its current share price of 15.20 EUR and given the current share count of 540.5M shares, Puig Brands is trading at an EV/EBITDA multiple of just over 9, adjusted for lease liabilities and lease amortization on both sides of the equation. That’s not expensive for a company focusing on luxury items.

I had obtained a small long position, but the recent market turmoil caught me by surprise and the stock is now trading approximately 20% lower. I think that’s an opportunity and the current fair value appears to be well north of 20 EUR per share given the company’s growth trajectory and robust balance sheet.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The More You Sell, The More I Buy: LyondellBasell
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

Imagine if you decided to never put in effort because it was difficult or painful, would you truly achieve anything worthwhile?

I hate running. It's miserable while you're engaged in the activity. Your legs ache for hours to days later, reminding you that you did it to yourself. Too much running can permanently damage your knees. Yet, if you ever want to complete a marathon, running is a must.

Running also provides many positive health benefits. Your heart gets stronger and healthier. It can promote a better body composition. It lets you eat more cookies without developing a gut.

As humans, we desire to avoid difficulty and seek out ease. We must force ourselves to experience difficulty to promote the best health or increased physical strength.

Likewise, governments may tread a path that creates discomfort for their citizens, believing it will produce a long-term positive result in the end. Tariffs can be one such measure. By increasing the cost of a good or service conducted overseas, the goal is to promote domestic production, which fills the void. In the short term, tariffs can create inflation as prices climb –corporations rarely absorb higher costs when they can pass them on – but in the long term, they can create a stronger self-reliant economy.

If the U.S. government is focused on boosting domestic manufacturing, then an important prerequisite is the increased availability of industrial feedstock. Over 95% of all manufactured goods are directly touched by chemistry and plastics – that is 70,000+ products, including food packaging, medicine, automotive parts, aerospace equipment, renewable energy, clean water, and much more. For a successful industrial society, chemical manufacturing is foundational.

However, the picture isn’t filled with roses for chemical manufacturers. The potential benefits of increased domestic demand are counterbalanced by challenges such as reduced export opportunities (due to retaliatory trade measures), and higher setup and operational costs.

This is why scale, margin of safety, liquidity, and adaptability are very important. Today, we will discuss a company that offers all of these and is positioned well to support more reshoring of manufacturing in America. We've highlighted them before, but the opportunity just got better and you shouldn't miss it.

An Inexpensive Global Leader

LyondellBasell Industries N.V. (NYSE:LYB), yielding 9.9%, is a global leader in the chemical industry and one of the world's largest producers of polymers. The company operates a highly diversified network of facilities across 32 countries, encompassing 94 manufacturing sites. The company has a significant global presence, with manufacturing sites and joint ventures in North America, South America, Europe, Asia Pacific, Africa, and the Middle East. In the United States specifically, the company maintains key facilities in Texas, Illinois, Ohio, and several other states, catering to the demand for chemicals and polymers. Source

This geographical diversification allows LyondellBasell to mitigate risks associated with regional economic fluctuations and trade policies.

Note: LYB is exiting the refinery business with the shutdown of the +100-year-old refinery in Houston due to significant losses over the years.

Strong Balance Sheet

LYB maintains an investment-grade balance sheet, ending FY 2024 with $3.4 billion in cash and short-term investments and $8 billion of available liquidity. The company generated $3.8 billion in cash from operations and returned $1.9 billion to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases. Notably, LYB has reduced its outstanding shares by ~31% over the past decade. The company maintains ample liquidity to support necessary expansions to cater to specific demand trends, and to make opportunistic acquisitions to expand its market presence in strategic locations.

During 2024, LYB invested $1.8 billion in capital expenditures and projected the figure to be $1.9 billion in 2025, broken down into $700 million for profitable growth projects and $1.2 billion on investments to maintain existing assets. The company continues to commit to returning cash to shareholders through growing dividends and share repurchases. We note that LYB has delivered growing dividends for 14 consecutive years (with three massive special payments), and is on-track to deliver another one on its next payment. The company’s dividend is typically 70% of its FCF (Free Cash Flow), and management confidently reiterated this during the quarterly announcement.

LyondellBasell continues to strategically review its less profitable segments (mainly in Europe), while investing in growth opportunities. A notable example is its acquisition of a 35% stake in National Petrochemical Industrial Company in Saudi Arabia, providing the company access to a key hub for petrochemical production. The Middle East, particularly Saudi Arabia, is a major hub for petrochemical production.

In addition, the company is a demonstrated leader in plastic waste recycling through its MoReTech platform, with commercial operations potentially beginning as early as next year, starting in Germany. The company maintains a strong balance sheet and liquidity to focus on and support the growth of its more profitable segments in the years ahead.

As the largest producer of polypropylene in North America, LYB benefits from its significant scale and diverse facilities across the U.S. This positions the company well to capitalize on increased domestic manufacturing activity, should such a trend materialize. Additionally, LYB has production facilities on every continent, allowing for pathways to domesticate the supply chain and cater to local markets.

From a valuation standpoint, LYB trades very cheaply at just 7.1x forward PE, making it one of the best times to be a buyer. The company provides a strong margin of safety amidst volatile market conditions, with a growing dividend, currently offering a 9.9% QDI yield.

Conclusion

To be an effective income investor, one must be willing to purchase what is on sale.

Rarely do high yields come from extremely popular, well-understood companies. High yields are usually the result of shifting sentiment, misunderstandings around a company's operations, or increasing risks to the company itself. LYB is massive and global in scale. This leads many to lack a deep understanding of what exactly it does. You don't go buy a bottle of LYB to drink on your drive home, like you do with Coke (KO). Furthermore, sentiment around global companies has shifted heavily as the Trump administration has caused significant confusion regarding its tariff policy. The shifting uncertainty makes it difficult for companies and investors to decide what positioning they should take to benefit the most. This shifting sentiment and confusion have led to LYB being on sale – trading at a largely discounted price. I'm happy to buy it.

LYB was the chicks at Tractor Supply (TSCO) a few years ago. Everyone looked past them because eggs were cheap and plentiful. Now people are clamoring over one another, desperate to buy chicks because eggs have become expensive and in demand.

Buy the chicks when everyone is overlooking them, and reap the rewards of a reliable supply of eggs!

data.title: Sailing Into A Bargain: Royal Caribbean's Post-Selloff Appeal
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

Shares of Royal Caribbean Cruises (RCL) have taken a hammering since my last coverage of the stock back in January. At the time, I argued that despite its strong operational performances, the stock appeared overextended with limited upside at 22 times forward earnings. However, with the choppiness in the market over the past three months, RCL stock has nosedived over 15%. Cruise stocks across the board felt the brunt of the pain, which peaked during the tariff fiasco. Moreover, with the higher recessionary risks, many believe things will continue to be dicey for cruise stocks. Though that’s true to an extent, I believe the market overreacted to the tariffs, especially given their likely minimal impact on cruises. On top of that, cruise liners, like RCL in particular, continue to execute superbly, marked by a healthy expansion in their bottom-line numbers. Hence, with RCL stock now trading below 13 times forward earnings, and while it could dip further, I believe the current price offers stellar upside, especially when factoring in its growing dividend, which adds to the overall package.

Innovation at Sea: The Royal Caribbean Blueprint

Royal Caribbean is a dominant force in the cruise line industry, commanding roughly 24.8% of the global market share. A big part of the company’s success has been its commitment to innovation, which has struck a chord with the younger demographic. To put things in perspective, roughly 50% of RCL’s passengers last year were Millennials or younger, which paved the way for a staggering 108.5% occupancy rate, beating both Carnival (CCL) and Norwegian (NCLH).

Moreover, its ability to innovate and enhance vacation experiences remains a major differentiator. Whether it is the Oasis class ships or the Icon of the Seas, the largest cruise ship in the world, RCL continues to refine its value proposition while expanding its customer base. This proactive approach was one of the key reasons why RCL recovered quicker than its peers while keeping its debt load in check. On top of that, unlike its peers, RCL continued to preserve shareholder value at a time when the cruise liners collectively amassed a whopping $74 billion in debt.

Q4 Gains, 2025 Outlook, and Strategic Growth Drivers

Royal Caribbean closed out 2024 with a bang, achieving its Trifecta financial goals 18 months ahead of schedule while posting impressive results. In Q4 alone, it generated north of $5 billion in operational cash flow, and 11.6% net yield expansion (constant currency), while delivering 8.6 million vacations.

Moreover, RCL wrapped up Q4 with remarkable YOY improvements across key metrics, underscoring sustained travel demand and healthy execution. It posted a $2.02 per share EPS or $600 million net income, roughly double the previous year’s $300 million, or $1.06 per share. Also, on an adjusted basis, EPS hit $1.63, comfortably ahead of analyst estimates of $1.50, along with total sales of $3.76 billion (13% higher on a YOY basis).

Gross margin yields jumped 13.8% in Q4, coinciding with a 108% load factor (2.9% higher YOY), which supports RCL’s strategy in filling ships above the typical two-per-berth thresholds. Additionally, Available Passenger Cruise Days (APCD), jumped 7.7% YOY, and an impressive 22% from pre-pandemic FY2019 levels.

I guess what stands out for RCL is its bottom-line strength, with it boasting a 17.45% net income margin, which essentially dwarfs the sector's 4.6%. Also, its return on common equity is at a powerful 46.83%, blowing ahead of the sector’s median at 309%, highlighting the company’s efficiency in reinvesting shareholder capital. Also, even though its dividend yield (1.56%) isn’t the most attractive, the recent increases, especially the latest 36.4% bump are also major positives.

As we look ahead, RCL expects 2025 to be another blowout year, projecting an adjusted EPS that falls in the $14.35 to $14.65 range (a midpoint of $14.50), above consensus estimates of $14.44. RCL’s CEO expects an upbeat 2025, projecting a 23% expected adjusted earnings expansion, crediting the “commercial and vacation experiences flywheel”. Overall, the goal is to continue working on brand equity, high-occupancy ships, and a greater focus on premium pricing.

Furthermore, RCL is looking to expand on its high-margin river cruising segment, particularly its Celebrity River brand. The goal is to adapt its winning formula by using smaller ships delivering more intimate and immersive experiences, with quicker investment returns. Moreover, RCL executives have already noted the potential for higher average pricing and yields. Each vessel can carry just 180 passengers, dropping capital outlays, while speeding up payoff timelines. With 10 ships slated for 2027, RCL sees its first order as just the tip of the iceberg. These expansions are effectively paired with belt-tightening measures while the synergy expected from ocean and river cruising boosts cross-selling opportunities.

Moreover, with the company’s superb results, we can see from the chart that its consensus outlook continues to improve, marked by a steady rise in EPS estimates and YOY growth projections. The 2025 consensus currently stands at $14.91, which reflects a heartening 26.39% annual increase. From there, analysts expect continued momentum, with EPS estimates rising to $17.58 by 2026 and $20.23 by the following year, maintaining mid-teens and impressive YOY growth rates. Perhaps what’s even more striking is the positive 6-month trend, where virtually all future forecasts have been revised upwards. For instance, 2025 EPS projections increased by approximately 4% over the past six months, while 2028 projections rose by over 20% in the same period.

Discussion of RCL Stock’s Valuation

As discussed earlier, the past few months have been mostly forgettable for RCL. From the chart, we can see that the stock climbed 16% in late January through early February, on the back of strong momentum and a positive earnings sentiment.

However, the bullish trend reversed emphatically, and from mid-February onwards, the stock plunged 38%, erasing previous gains and then some. The plunge has left the stock trading comfortably below both key short-term moving averages.

The first hurdle for the stock at this point is to reclaim its 20-day EMA, which could highlight a potential change in short-term sentiment. Once it can go past the 20-day EMA at $204.53, the next target will be to test the $218.48 area (the 50-day EMA).

Moreover, we can also see that the Relative Strength Index [RSI] index rating for the stock has effectively tanked, from the high-70s to currently around the 42 mark. Hence, the depressed RSI reading along with a prolonged stay beneath key moving averages suggests the stock is remarkably oversold.

Furthermore, the snapshot above exemplifies the stock’s current oversold conditions. The stock’s forward PE ratio non-GAAP is at 12.91, below the 13.23 sector median, and a stark contrast to its January levels. Also, its PEG ratio stands at a considerably low 0.24, which is again significantly lower than the sector median at 0.63. Additionally, its EV-to-sales multiple of 4.39 is higher than the sector benchmark but is roughly 88% lower than its 5-year average.

Risks To The Thesis

Despite the attractive entry point and healthy operational momentum, there are multiple risks that challenge RCL’s bull thesis.

Perhaps the most obvious risk is the current macroeconomic backdrop, with recession discussions back on the table and higher-for-longer borrowing rates potentially weighing on discretionary spending. Though RCL’s management has displayed commendable discipline in managing costs, any drop in consumer sentiment could have a material impact on forward bookings and pricing power.

On top of that, its management has set lofty expectations, which leaves virtually no room for error in execution. Additionally, the recent tariff-driven market selloff sheds light on the sector’s vulnerability to policy shocks and broader market volatility. Hence, with these factors in mind, it’s important to consider that the near-term headwinds could potentially disrupt the otherwise robust long-term trajectory for RCL.

Bottom-line On RCL Stock

Royal Caribbean's stock is ripe for the picking following its recent selloff, with current pricing metrics offering stellar upside potential. The cruise giant benefits from robust consumer demand, that's held up in adverse circumstances. On top of that, with an improved balance sheet, expanded offerings, and an eye-catching bottom-line positioning, RCL remains a top bet in its niche. However, risks remain, including potential economic headwinds that could curb discretionary spending, while fluctuating fuel costs continue to pressure operating margins. Nonetheless, with disciplined execution and vigorous booking trends, RCL’s fundamentals and technical setup support a bullish outlook for patient investors.

data.title: UroGen: June 13 PDUFA For Potential Bladder Cancer Blockbuster Sets Up Strong Buy
data.description:
This is my fifth UroGen Pharma (NASDAQ:URGN) article, following 11/2024's "UroGen Pharma: Choice Based On Near-Term High-Value PDUFA" (High-Value). In this article, I will again admonish myself for a strong miss in High-Value's “Strong Buy” rating insofar as it has endured a ~20% drop in the intervening time.

Stubborn to the core, I am a recidivist here; I maintain my “Strong Buy” albeit with a cautionary note. One must recognize the extreme risk facing today's markets; this risk is especially sharp for more speculative names, such as early commercial-stage biotechs like UroGen.

This article's updated assessment relies on UroGen's earnings and information as reported in its 3/10/2025 Q4, 2025 earnings press release, conference call and 10-K. The premise of this article is that UroGen's ~$0.43 billion market cap fails to recognize the value of its near-term UGN-102 (mitomycin) pivotal trial results catalyst.

The FDA's recent acceptance of UroGen's NDA and issuance of an 06/13/2025 PDUFA has set the stage. As I write on 04/10/2025 it is now an ~2 months wait for an FDA decision on UGN-102 (mitomycin) in treatment of low-grade intermediate-risk non-muscle invasive bladder cancer (LG-IR-NMIBC).

The call provides late data from the pivotal trial supporting UroGen's NDA.

One can never reliably predict how the FDA will rule on any given application. That has never been more true than now with the current personnel reshuffling resulting from new political leadership. However, UroGen's UGN-102 NDA makes a strong case for approval. The call provides an interesting and helpful discussion of UroGen's ENVISION phase 3 trial results, which it is using to support its NDA.

ENVISION bears clinicaltrials.gov ID — NCT05243550. It had an enrollment of 240 patients. The treatment specified for the study was six-weekly intravesical instillations of UGN-102. Patients were directed to return to the clinic ~6 months following first treatment for assessment of their response.

The assessment protocol was convoluted; it read as follows:

Patients who had a complete response (CR) at the 3-month visit, defined as having no detectable disease in the bladder, entered the follow-up period of the study. Patients who had a non-complete response (NCR) due to residual LG disease underwent investigator-designated standard of care (SOC) treatment of remaining lesions and then entered the follow-up period of the study.

During the follow-up period, patients return to the clinic every three months for up to 24 months (i.e., 27 months after the first instillation) for evaluation of response. Patients who remain disease-free at the 27-month visit will continue to be followed every six months for up to 36 months (i.e., 63 months after the first instillation) or until disease recurrence, disease progression, death, or the study is closed by the sponsor, whichever occurs first.

Patients who had a disease recurrence during the follow-up period or a disease progression at any time underwent investigator-designated SOC treatment and had a separate End of Study (EOS) visit performed. The timing of the EOS visit was approximately three months after SOC treatment of disease recurrence or progression.

During the call, CEO Barrett advised of the following ENVISION trial results:

  1. The CR rate at three months stood at 79.6%;

  2. The 12-month duration of response was 82.3% Kaplan-Meier analysis;

  3. As the data continues to mature, today (3/10/2025) we are reporting updated 18-month duration of response data from ENVISION and note that these results remain consistent with previous Kaplan-Meier estimate;

  4. With 102 patients now (3/10/2025) followed for at least 18 months following CR, or 21 months post-therapy start, the updated duration of response is 80.6% by Kaplan-Meier analysis.

She also advised that UroGen had recently discussed this data with the FDA during its midcycle review of the NDA. It plans to refer the filing to its Oncologic Drugs Advisory Committee (ODAC). This will be a crucial decision point for UGN-102, although occasionally the FDA will overrule ODAC decisions.

During the call, CMO Schoenberg noted that it was all hands-on deck in preparation for the ODAC. He noted that UGN-102 offered a much-needed paradigm shift from the current transurethral resection of bladder tumor (TURBT) procedure that has been SOC for decades. He advised:

... Urologists appreciate the need for alternative treatment options that could potentially provide a longer treatment and recurrence-free survival, and UGN-102 has the potential to fill this gap, offering office-based administration that aligns with the way urologists practice.

Expectations following launch are important reality checks.

J-code issues

I am excited about the potential for rapid uptake of the revolutionary change in treatment ushered in by UGN-102. Management is optimistic but recognizes the logistic issues inherent in its launch. Assuming the FDA gives UGN-102 approval on 06/13/2025, it will initially be assigned a miscellaneous J-code for billing purposes. It will likely not have a permanent specific J-code until 01/2026.

In response to a question during the call, CCO Lin advised that the assumed practical impact of this was:

...that the overall reimbursement process [will take] a little bit longer. So, somewhere around the 50 days to 60 days for a provider to be reimbursed. And that number does cut down by about half when we have a unique permanent miscellaneous J-code...

CEO Barrett then pointed out that physicians would generally be uncomfortable with such longer delays in their office practice. One workaround she mentioned was to move the procedure to the hospital.

Launch trajectory

In response to a question during the call as to the trajectory of the launch rollout sales, CCO Lin said that it should be similar to that of its previously approved product JELMYTO. He was careful to distinguish between the shape of the trajectory and the absolute numbers because the population of eligible patients is larger for UGN-102 than it is for JELMYTO.

Slide 12 of UroGen's 03/25 Presentation titled "Developing Innovative Medicines to Treat Urothelial Cancers" shows JELMYTO's revenue trajectory:

JELMYTO's FDA approval came on 04/2020. Revenues that year were minimal. UGN-102 seems destined for a similar trajectory, albeit with higher starting numbers. Investors should temper expectations for UGN-102's 2025 revenues, even if it is approved right on schedule.

Sales reps

Surprisingly, UroGen is only planning a modest increase in sales reps from its current 52 to 83 to handle sales of its combined JELMYTO and UGN-102. During the call an analyst asked how this would work given the guided $1 billion in peak UGN-102 sales, which is ~10X that expected for JELMYTO.

CCO Lin's response to the question was hard to follow. He noted:

...our analysis to increase the size of our commercial footprint from about 52 territories to around 83 territories are really hinged on patient epidemiology. And one important thing to note is that with JELMYTO and the low-grade UTUC with something like 6,000 patients in a year that might present, the likelihood of any one urologist seeing more than one of these is fairly uncommon. In the case of low-grade intermediate-risk NMIBC, what we hear from customers is that they are seeing they know who these patients are.

And the vast majority of urology practices do see these patients. And so, the sizing of 83 territories allows us to get to the vast majority of treating physicians and practices. So, it's something like roughly 85% of the market is covered. So, we are really hitting the sweet spot in learning from our experiences with JELMYTO and that the frequency of our visits does make a difference. And so, we're increasing that.

Perhaps recognizing the deficits to this answer, CEO Barrett pitched in to the discussion; she noted that beyond the 82 sales reps, UroGen also had additional personnel supporting the sales reps. These include:

  1. nurse educators;
  2. medical science liaisons for peer-to-peer support,
  3. operations managers that help with the operational lift and logistics;
  4. account directors; and
  5. field reimbursement managers.

In other words, it takes a village to fully handle the sales function. Barrett did not want to risk leaving the impression that UroGen had not fully thought thru the critical customer support activities.

Liquidity should be sufficient to support the initial phase of the UGN-102 launch.

During the call CFO Degnan reported that as of 12/31/2024 UroGen had $241.7 million in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities. Its annual cash burn as reflected by its 10-K 2024 net cash (used in) provided by operating activities was ~$97 million; this was up from ~$76 million for 2023.

In terms of cash runway, Degnan advised that UroGen was:

...well capitalized to execute our operating plan and advance the company to and through profitability with the anticipated launch of UGN-102 later this year.

Despite my “Strong Buy” rating, UroGen presents a significant risk of loss for shareholders.

One could write a lengthy book listing the risks attendant upon investment in UroGen. The risks section of the 10-K does just that. I will try to focus on the three risks that are the most pressing:

  1. timely FDA approval of UGN-102 with broad label;
  2. successful marketing of UGN-102;
  3. Intellectual property.

In regard to FDA approval, that is certainly a risk with which biotech investors are well familiar. I will not dwell on it here. The label question is specific to UGN-102. During the call, Barrett emphasized that Urogen was focusing on recurrent disease. The 10-K describes the situation as:

.... We estimate that the annual treatable population of low-grade intermediate-risk NMIBC in the United States is approximately 82,000, of which approximately ... 59,000 are estimated to be recurrent patients. We estimate that the total addressable market opportunity for UGN-102 in low-grade intermediate-risk NMIBC is potentially over $5.0 billion, assuming an expected pricing range of $16,000 to $19,000 per dose.

As for successful marketing, that is often a challenge for biotechs with limited resources and experience, such as UroGen. It remains to be seen whether UroGen's planned 82 sales reps with backup as described above provide effective marketing for UGN-102. I expect that a strong DTC program will be necessary to maximize its potential.

On page 12 of the 10-K, it describes the complicated matrix of possible competitors to UGN-102. Presentation slides 15 provides a brief overview of the situation, noting that the SOC is repetitive TURBT; also, that UGN-102 is furthest along in clinical development as a non-surgical chemoablative therapy. Of course, if the FDA delays its approval, it could be leapfrogged.

UGN-102's intellectual property rights are complex. Its patent protection is described at great length in its 10-K. Unfortunately, it is like a will-o'-the-wisp in terms of the duration of key protections for UGN-102.

Conclusion

In today's political environment, with tariffs whipsawing stocks and cutbacks shuffling government agencies, it is daunting to peg a “Strong Buy” rating on a biotech awaiting an FDA decision. Nonetheless, that is my view on UroGen. Its meager market cap of ~$0.43 billion compared to the ~$1 billion peak sales potential of UGN-102 if approved sets up an attractive potential for gain of up to 6-10X depending on the multiplier one uses.

data.title: Richmond Mutual Bancorporation: Not Attractive Enough Despite 5% Yield
data.description:
As I’ve covered in previous articles, I see Richmond Mutual Bancorporation (RMBI) as a small regional bank that doesn’t have any competitive advantage within the banking sector, being its relatively high-dividend yield the most attractive feature of its investment case.

Not surprisingly, its shares have not performed particularly well in recent months, pushing its dividend yield closer to 5%, compared to around 3.8% some months ago. Given this higher yield and the fact that since my previous analysis the bank has reported its annual earnings related to 2024, in this article I review its most recent earnings and update its investment case, to see if it currently offers more value or not for long-term investors. In 2024, the bank’s operating performance was stable compared to the previous year, as the bank’s operations didn’t change much, but higher costs were a headwind for earnings growth.

Richmond is a retail-oriented bank and its core business is mainly taking deposits and making loans, being quite simple within the banking sector. Due to this business profile, the bank is highly geared to interest rates given that the vast majority of revenues come from net interest income [NII].

During 2024, the Federal Reserve maintained its key rate unchanged until September, while after that, it made three rate cuts, which were a headwind for revenue growth over the last couple of quarters for Richmond and other banks highly reliant on NII.

Despite that, Richmond was able to report higher average asset yields, which increased to 5.55% (vs. 4.98% in 2023) in 2024. This represents an increase of 57 basis points (bps) on average asset yields, which led to higher interest income in the year (+19% YoY to $80 million). Its loan book also increased, by 6.2% YoY to $1.16 billion.

On the liabilities side, Richmond had to increase its average cost of deposits, which is its main funding source, having a negative impact on the net interest margin (NIM) – which is the difference between asset and liability yields. Richmond’s average cost of deposits increased to around 2.9% in 2024, representing an increase of 50 bps compared to the previous year, while its total deposits increased by 5% to $1.09 billion.

Additionally, other liabilities also reported higher funding costs, leading to an average rate paid on interest-bearing liabilities of 3.37%, representing an increase of 78 bps during 2024. This increase was higher than compared to asset yields, explaining why Richmond’s NIM declined by 11 bps during last year to 2.67%. While this is still an acceptable interest spread, it’s much lower than compared to previous years due to higher costs of deposits following the regional banking turmoil in 2023.

Indeed, Richmond’s average NIM was about 3.50% from 2015-2022, while over the past couple of years it declined to around 2.70%, showing that Richmond’s business was quite impacted by increasing risk-aversion toward regional banks following the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank. While Richmond was able to protect its deposit base and its reputation in Indiana remains good, customers demand much higher rates to keep their money with the bank than they used to, being a structural headwind for the bank’s profitability.

As Richmond’s core business is basically taking deposits and use this funding to provide loans, higher cost of deposits had a great impact on its profitability level and this is something that it’s difficult to reverse in the near future.

The bank acknowledges this issue and its strategy has been to push for other banking products and other alternatives like insurance, aiming to grow revenues coming from fees, but so far, this has not changed much of its revenue mix. Indeed, NII represented about 89% of total revenues in 2024, a similar weight compared to the previous year, showing that its revenues are highly reliant on rates and this profile is likely to be maintained over the coming years. While non-interest income increased at a slightly higher rate than NII during the last year (3.2% vs. 2.8%), this was not enough to change much of the revenue mix.

Regarding costs, its total expenses increased by 4.3% YoY to $32.1 million, representing an absolute increase of $1.3 million compared to the previous year. This development was justified by increases in salaries and employee benefits, and other administrative expenses, leading to an efficiency ratio of 73.7% in 2024. This is not a great efficiency level in the banking sector given that peers with better efficiency report ratios between 50-60%. However, I don’t expect Richmond to improve much in this ratio in the near future, given that its business model is highly reliant on its branch network and also due to its small size. Moreover, as inflation has remained somewhat sticky in recent years, it seems that wage pressure is likely to remain a headwind for higher costs ahead, not boding well for an improvement on the bank’s efficiency levels in the coming years.

Regarding credit quality, it remained quite good, showing that Richmond does not take much credit risk into its balance sheet, explaining why its provisions for loan losses remained at very low levels in recent years. During 2024, provisions were only $0.5 million, practically unchanged from the previous year, and its non-performing loans ratio declined to 0.58% (vs. 0.72% in 2023), being a very low level. While recent drama about Trump’s tariffs increases the risk of an economic recession in the near future, Richmond has low exposure to consumer credit and most of its loan book is covered by mortgages, and thus credit losses are expected to remain low over the next few years.

Due to this combination of slightly higher revenues, stable provisions for loan losses, but higher operating expenses during the last year, Richmond’s net income was $9.4 million in 2024, being flat compared to the previous year. This shows that Richmond’s growth prospects are quite low and this is not expected to change much during 2025, as the market is still expecting the Fed to make more rate cuts, with lower rates being a headwind for Richmond’s NII and earnings growth in 2025.

Regarding its profitability, the bank’s return on equity (ROE) ratio was only 7.03% last year (vs. 7.36% in 2023), which is a relatively low level of profitability in the banking sector and is below the bank’s cost of equity. This means that Richmond is currently not generating enough returns to compensate its shareholders for the risk, which theoretically should lead to a valuation below book value.

However, despite some recent share price weakness, Richmond is currently trading at about 0.97x book value, which seems somewhat high for a bank with its business profile and level of profitability. As I’ve analyzed in a previous article, I see a fair valuation around 0.85x book value, and thus its shares appear to remain overvalued.

Regarding its capital position and shareholder remuneration, Richmond reported a slightly higher CET1 ratio at the end of 2024 (12.98% vs. 12.85% in 2023), showing that its organic capital generation is enough to distribute dividends to shareholders and still build up a little bit its capital position. This happens because its dividend payout ratio is relatively conservative, as the bank distributed about 60% of its earnings to shareholders through dividends during 2024.

More recently, the bank has decided to raise its quarterly dividend by 7% to $0.15 per share, or $0.60 per share annually, being the first quarterly increase since March 2023. At its current share price, this leads to a forward dividend yield slightly above 5%, which seems to be attractive for long-term investors and is one of the most interesting features of its investment case.

Conclusion

Richmond Mutual Bancorporation offers a relatively high-dividend yield compared to other banks in the U.S., but in my opinion, this is not enough to make its investment case attractive enough for long-term investors. The bank’s growth prospects are relatively low, and its business profile is not expected to change much ahead, while potential lower rates are expected to be a significant headwind for earnings growth in 2025, not making Richmond much attractive right now.

data.title: Apple: The Bullish Thesis Is Breaking Down, But I'm Buying (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Apple (AAPL) has recovered alongside the rest of tech peers and the broader market, but it isn’t clear whether the worst is over. The stock has long traded at rich valuations which I have at times previously justified as being due to the company’s rock-solid growth story. That growth story is now facing great hurdles in the near to medium term, as the tariff war has led to the possibility of heightened manufacturing costs. The stock is admittedly not that cheap even after the recent pullback, but this is looking like an attractive entry point in what still remains one of the top businesses in the world. There may be great volatility in the near term for both profits and the stock price, but it is time to upgrade the stock to a buy rating.

AAPL Stock Price

I last covered AAPL in December where I downgraded the stock due to valuation and disappointment regarding the generative AI rollout. The stock has declined around 20% since then.

This decline comes as the company faces new headwinds that may have real impacts on the underlying financials, but the long-term growth story remains intact.

AAPL Stock Key Metrics

The latest quarter saw the company post mild top and bottom-line growth with gross margins rising to 80 bps mainly due to the rapidly growing services business.

Rolling out generative AI has not led to a projected super-cycle in iPhone sales - in fact, iPhone sales declined 0.7% YoY. The company has seen growth rates come to a crawl for many years now, making it the slowest grower among mega-cap tech names. The jewel of the business is clearly the services segment which saw revenues grow 14% YoY in the quarter.

The company ended the quarter with $141 billion of cash versus $97 billion in debt, representing a solid net cash balance sheet.

On the conference call, management guided for the upcoming quarter to see “low to mid-single-digit” overall revenue growth with “low-double-digits” growth in the services business. Management did note that the iPhone 16 saw stronger results in areas where they had rolled out Apple Intelligence. As an iPhone user myself, I can relate to the anecdotal notes that Apple Intelligence has been rather underwhelming, and I would not be surprised if AAPL were losing some market share as a result of the rise of generative AI. That said, I expect the company to eventually catch up, as it has often succeeded from having a second-mover-advantage as evidenced by its success in items like the Apple Watch and AirPods, two items which I anecdotally note were also underwhelming in their initial releases.

Is AAPL Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

At first glance, it might seem curious to be upgrading AAPL here. The stock does not trade that cheaply at 26x earnings.

Consensus estimates call for mid-single-digit revenue growth moving forward, and these estimates have not yet factored in the impact of tariffs, which are likely to hurt demand.

Wedbush analyst Dan Ives has estimated that it will not be until 2028 at the earliest if AAPL were to move its production to the US and even then, the listing cost of the iPhone would still soar. I share the consensus view that this is a very unlikely outcome.

That said, I do not share the view that AAPL might get a “specific company exemption” as that would greatly take away any negotiating leverage. Other analysts have projected tariffs to lead to as much as 28% declines in earnings per share. It is unclear how the analysts came up with this estimate, as it is reported that China accounts for 80% of the production capacity. My naive calculation of simply doubling production expenses would lead to operating profits being completely wiped out. I suspect that the actual impact would be somewhere in between the analyst estimate and this nightmare scenario. While analysts have appeared to primarily focus on the cases where the company either eats the production costs, raises sticker prices, or some combination of both, I would also note the possibility that AAPL might seek to simply delay production and hope that the tariff disputes are resolved in a timely fashion. This would, of course, come at the risk of losing market share.

Following the 90-day delay on tariffs on every country other than China, I am now of the view that the base case scenario is for AAPL to slowly move its production out of China and lean more into its existing footprints in Vietnam and India. There are risks here, too. Not only would this take a great deal of time - similar to Ives’ analysis of a move to the US - but it might be putting its business in China at risk, which recently stood at around 15% of overall sales.

This analysis all would appear to point to difficult times ahead for the company, and I have not even yet mentioned the likelihood that consensus estimates prove too optimistic for this year (and perhaps even 2026) as the market digests the slower economic activity as a result of the tariff fights. Yet, the key here is that the long-term drivers of the services business remain intact, as AAPL effectively acts as a toll road on its ecosystem. The services business had a 75% gross margin and may go higher over time. Some investors might point out that many tech names have higher margins, but the main point here is that this services business better resembles Visa (V) and less so a subscription software business (again, “toll road”). I project long-term margins to hit 50% at the very least and this might end up still being too conservative (I acknowledge that it might seem ambitious right now). At a 2x price to earnings growth ratio (‘PEG ratio’), that would place this segment at around 30x earnings, equating to 15x sales or a $1.8 trillion valuation. That leaves roughly $1 trillion of value for the products business lines, for a roughly 11x earnings multiple. Sure, this segment is about to experience great volatility, and it has substantially more cyclicality than the services business. Yet due to AAPL’s sticky ecosystem, I see this segment growing at a 3% pace or better over the long term, as the company should be able to grow through a combination of increasing its user base, increasing its product adoption, and price increases. Even this more cyclical products business is deserving of a premium valuation, but if we assume a conservative 15x earnings multiple, then that would place the valuation at around $1.5 trillion. Summed together, we arrive at a target stock price of $225 per share.

AAPL Stock Key Risks

AAPL stock is likely to be very volatile. I question why the stock recovered alongside peers given its high manufacturing concentration in China. The stock is not so cheap and can re-rate lower. Management’s commitment to returning capital to shareholders offers some downside support, but it might take time to re-build investor confidence in the long-term growth story (assuming this confidence is lost). It is possible that either China or the US target the company given its large size.

AAPL Stock Conclusion

AAPL faces a perfect storm. I hesitate to call this a “generational opportunity” as the valuation is admittedly not that attractive relative to long-term growth rates. Yet, I continue to view this business as having strong moats due to the sticky ecosystem. I expect the services business to eventually achieve top-tier margins similar to payment processing companies. Investors rarely have opportunities to buy AAPL at reasonable valuations - I suspect that this is one of those times. I can hardly fault readers if they skip on the stock due to there being cheaper opportunities elsewhere, I too have allocated heavier to more attractively valued tech names in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am upgrading the stock to a buy rating.

data.title: IGIB: Another Investment Grade Portfolio Hedge
data.description:
For investors who want to tactically or maybe strategically diversify into bonds, there are a number of very good exchange-traded funds to do this. This article will cover the iShares 5-10 Year Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (NASDAQ:IGIB) and follows on from my recent articles on the iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG) and the iShares 10+ Year Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (IGLB).

Corporate Bond Basics

Feel free to skip this brief section if you know about bonds. The reason bonds are defensive is because they are higher up the capital structure. Legally, for companies, the interest on bonds must be paid before dividends can be paid. Bondholders don't participate in the growth of the company (unless we are talking about convertible bonds), so they have less upside in good times. But because they always get paid first, they are less exposed to the downturns in the economy. That's why equity investors tend to focus on income statements because net income and earnings per share will dictate dividend amounts and reinvestment rates, whereas bondholders typically focus on the balance sheet, asking if a company is solvent and has enough liquidity to repay debt and debt interest. As the saying goes, bondholders are not looking for a return on capital, but a return of capital.

Not all bonds are equal; of course, US Treasuries are backed by the full faith and credit of the US Government, and because the government can always print money, these bonds can always be repaid. Corporates obviously can't print money, but if they have a "fortress balance sheet" with low levels of debt relative to total capital and decent profitability, they will generally be classified as Investment Grade, which in the rating world is anything up to BBB from S&P, or BAA from Moody's. Non-investment grade, aka high yield, aka junk bonds, trade more like equity because debt repayment is less secure.

Long-duration bonds are riskier, in terms of sensitivity to interest rates, than short duration bonds, due to the time value of money aspect. Bonds are typically issued for 5, 10, 20 or 30 years. Equities, however, in theory, offer dividends in perpetuity, so these cash flows are even more sensitive to interest rates, not to mention the fact that the equity risk premium is higher than the debt risk premium given equity's place in the corporate capital structure.

It's for these reasons that I'm turning my attention to investment-grade bonds. While the tariff headlines are constantly changing, it seems to me there is a real risk of recession, and I think the Fed will have to cut interest rates later in the year. Even if Trump does a U-turn on China tariffs, which isn't my base case scenario, the uncertainty around economic policies is causing consumer and business sentiment to weaken.

Which bonds?

I previously wrote about AGG, which has a duration of about 5.8 years and an equity beta of 0.32—this ETF is at the lower end of the Investment Grade risk spectrum due to its large holding of US Treasuries and its mix of short-term, medium-term and long-term maturity bond holdings. IGLB, which I wrote about here, while still investment grade, holds no US Government securities and holds bonds with longer maturities, and so its duration is 12 years and its equity beta is 0.71.

What about if you don't want exposure to US Treasuries because you want to capture a higher yield, but you also want lower risk than IGLB? That's where the iShares 5-10 Year Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF or IGIB comes in to play.

IGIB Portfolio Characteristics

IGIB tracks the ICE Bank of America 5-10 Year US Corporate Index, meaning the ETF holds intermediate-term U.S. investment-grade corporate bonds. Like its iShares peers, it has a very low expense ratio of 0.04%. It has an effective duration of 6 years and an equity beta of 0.39. This means IGIB is a little bit riskier than AGG but quite a bit less risky than IGLB.

Due to the lack of government securities, IGIB takes on more credit risk than AGG, and so has a higher yield to maturity of 5.38% vs. AGG's 4.63%. Just under 94% of its exposure is to A- or BBB-rated bonds.

In terms of sector exposure, 24.6% is banking, 12.4% is Consumer Non-Cyclical, 8.4% is Technology and 8.1% is Electric Utilities, with the rest of the portfolio widely spread between other sectors.

Seeking Alpha ETF Grades

Like its sister products, IGIB scores well for Expenses, and Liquidity (both A+), but it also scores well for Risk (A+), due to its relatively low all-round risk and momentum at A-. For dividends, the ETF is rated at D+, but again, the fund pays a distribution on a monthly basis. The Seeking Alpha Quant Rating is also Buy.

Risks

Compared to IGLB, has a larger weighting to banks and other more cyclical sectors, so this does add an element of greater exposure to an economic downturn. However, this is offset in my opinion by the fact that this ETF is lower in risk from an interest rate sensitivity perspective (lower duration), and the ETF has a lower equity beta than IGLB too.

Just like IGLB, the risk to this investment, especially if this is a tactical portfolio move, is that inflation kicks in and the Fed deems it non-transitory, and then doesn't cut interest rates this year, meanwhile, if the economy did weaken, credit spreads would likely widen, meaning yields on the fund's bonds would go up, and the bond portfolio would go down in value.

The ETF holds around 2,900 securities and has assets under management of $14.8 billion, making it one of the larger fixed-income ETFs in the market. This helps with the liquidity to trade in and out of the ETF and helps keep the expense ratio low.

Summary

This fixed income ETF is an ideal choice if you are nervous about the US economy's trajectory, but want to earn income while you move some assets out of equities, but you don't want to have your bond portfolio have too high a sensitivity to interest rate moves (duration risk) but at the same time you want to earn a higher yield than you would from AGG given that fund's relatively high weighting to government securities.

data.title: Krispy Kreme: Upside From Here Is Much More Attractive
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Krispy Kreme (NASDAQ:DNUT), which I recommended a buy rating given the solid execution in the partnership with McDonald’s, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. I don’t think the sharp sell-off over the past few months was justified. DNUT fundamentals remain solid in my view, and the growth drivers are still intact. As such, I reiterate a buy rating.

Investment thesis

One of the most important things to track for DNUT is whether its Delivered Fresh Daily (DFD) strategy is moving in the right direction, and the reality is, it is. The latest numbers we have from the Q4 '24 results are that DNUT has generated >$100 million in global DFD revenue (this is >25% of Q4 '24 revenue). This is the first time DNUT has crossed that threshold in a single quarter, and I think this validates that the hub-and-spoke (H&S) model works. This has a major implication on the growth outlook of the DNUT business because it is now a matter of whether DNUT can scale this H&S strategy. My view is that, yes, DNUT can do this, and the numbers are supportive of my view. In just ~3 years, DNUT has almost doubled its DFD doors from ~8.6k in FY21 to ~15.5k in FY24, and notably, in Q4 '24 itself, it opened 1.7k new doors, which is more than the entire opening in FY22 (~1.3k) and just a little short of the amount it opened in FY23 (~2k).

Moving forward, I believe the pace of opening will continue at this pace as DNUT can leverage its national partners (such as McDonald’s, Kroger, Publix, and Target) reach and existing infrastructure. Especially with McDonald’s, we know the partnership continues to work very well as management effectively guided for a steep acceleration in rollout, from >1,900 doors in Q4 '24 to 6,000 doors by 2025.

If we look beyond the US, DNUT has also executed very well on the international front. International organic revenue grew 7.8% y/y in Q4 '24, an acceleration from the last two quarters, and is supported by 14% growth in international points of access. Notably, the international DFD business saw 21% y/y growth. The appetite for DNUT international franchises also does not seem to be slowing down since its franchise partner in France, which has already opened 19 shops in Paris, plans to add 50 more points of access in 2025.

At the same time, I thought it was timely that DNUT is formally evaluating the refranchising of several mature international markets, such as the UK, Australia, Mexico, Canada, and Japan. This is a strategic shift I see as highly value-accretive. Firstly, this would provide DNUT with an influx of cash, which is extremely handy now considering it has ~$872 million in net debt (4.5x net debt to EBITDA ratio). Not only would DNUT be able to pay off a substantial amount of debt, but it also gives it more flexibility to press hard on expanding its DFD doors. Secondly, DNUT will shift towards a more asset-light model that has higher margins and a more predictable revenue stream.

More on margins: outside of this refranchising exercise, there are also other factors that, I think, will drive more margin expansion. One of them is management's decision to outsource >50% of US DFD logistics by the end of 2025. So far, the pilot programs have been noted to be successful in that third-party carriers delivered strong service levels and cost predictability. This is a dramatic shift and one that is very strategic because the key growth driver is the DFD, which is heavily reliant on a strong logistical network that is costly to maintain and build. By transitioning away from DNUT’s owned fleet, DNUT should be able to reduce volatility in delivery costs and improve scheduling efficiency. These two factors directly ease the SG&A burden as volume scales.

Valuation

DNUT’s valuation has gotten to a very attractive level. When I wrote my update in November 2024, the stock traded at ~11x forward EBITDA, and it now trades at just 8.5x. I don’t see any structural weakness since then; instead, as I noted above, I believe the growth momentum remains intact and the drivers are still very much in place. I understand some investors may be worried about the tariffs causing a potential recession, but Trump has shown willingness to reverse his plans, so I don’t think one should heavily discount DNUT’s valuation based on this tariff uncertainty. Even if I were to use a very conservative assumption, I think the upside is still very attractive. For instance, management is guiding for adjusted EBITDA of $315 million in FY26, and assuming multiples stay at 8x, which is still a heavy discount from the average of 14x, there is a >100% upside from the current share price.

Risk

Although the growth pathway is clear, the problem lies in whether DNUT can continue to execute at this high level. The area that I suspect could potentially be a problem is the logistic aspect, which DNUT is now outsourcing. While I see the benefits, this also means lower quality assurance, and if the provider messes up, it could really hurt the growth momentum of DNUT. Additionally, the international refranchising plan depends on the macroeconomy—franchisees appetite for taking on risk. This may not happen if the macroenvironment gets worse.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for DNUT is still a buy. Despite the recent share price weakness, I believe the upside remains attractive as underlying fundamentals remain robust. The DFD strategy is clearly gaining traction, both domestically and internationally. Operationally, DNUT is also making smart decisions like offloading logistics and refranchising mature markets to improve scalability and margin profile. With valuation at lows, if management can achieve its EBITDA guidance, I think the risk-reward skew is now heavily in favor of the upside.

data.title: 5 Reasons To Buy British American Tobacco
data.description:
In a year when the stock markets are sagging, British American Tobacco (BTI) or BAT for short, stands out, with a 9% increase year-to-date [YTD] compared with a 15% decline in the S&P 500 (SP500). But with an increase in its price comes the question - is the stock still attractive?

It certainly looked good when I last checked on it in December last year, resulting in a Buy rating on it. And as it turns out, there are still plenty of reasons that make a case for retaining the rating. Here are five of them.

#1. Defensives for uncertain times

The table below shows that all equity sectors have seen a contraction YTD. But of these, defensives like consumer staples, healthcare and utilities have seen the smallest declines. Sub-segments of consumer staples, like tobacco, have done even better.

Stocks like Philip Morris (PM) and Imperial Brands (OTCQX:IMBBY) have seen even bigger increases than BTI, of 26% and 15% YTD respectively. This is hardly a coincidence. It just shows a flight to safety as the times get increasingly uncertain.

The prospects of the US economy, in particular, are facing a question mark owing to the tariff changes imposed by the current administration. Banks like JPMorgan (JPM) now see 60% odds of a recession this year, while other forecasters have also raised the probabilities of a recession.

Even if a recession doesn't quite happen, the uncertainty can still damage some of the economic performance of the economy, after some softening was already expected earlier. If not an outright recession, a slowdown looks increasingly possible at this stage. It follows then that staples, and tobacco stocks in particular, can stay in favour.

#2. Dividends over capital gains

At a time when more careful stock picking than usual is required to make capital gains, dividends offer a nice alternative for assured returns. And tobacco stocks, with relatively high dividend yields, are at an advantage here.

Even among tobacco stocks, though, BTI looks good. At 7.6%, its forward dividend yield is second only to IMBBY, which is at 7.8%. While the company's dividend payout ratio, at 66.3%, for 2024 is on the high side, the fact that it has made payments for 14 consecutive years gives some assurance. Also, analysts estimate that it will see a marginal 1% increase in earnings per share [EPS] this year, which might not mean much for a dividend increase but certainly indicates potential dividend stability.

#3. Profit looks good despite settlement provisions

The last time I checked, there was a risk to the company's profits owing to a pending settlement of lawsuits in Canada. But the risk was successfully met. Even though the company made a provision of GBP 6.2 billion for the same in 2024, its profits looked good. The reported operating profit, at GBP 27.4 billion, actually turned out way better than in 2023 because one-off impairment charges then had resulted in a loss.

Adjusted operating profits were marginally lower (see table below) and the adjusted net profit was impacted slightly more, as it fell by 4% to GBP 8.1 billion, though they were spared of the provisions. Overall though, the profit picture looks alright.

#4. New categories make gains

The company also continues to pivot towards tobacco free products, which provide some assurance of its future at a time when tobacco products are losing popularity. This is evident from the fact that the company anticipates a 2% decline in the global tobacco industry's volumes in 2025.

By contrast, consumers of smokeless products, including traditional oral ones, are growing as reflected in a 14% increase in the same for BAT in 2024. This is also seen in the solid growth in the new categories segment during the year (see chart below).

The segment's revenue share rose to 13.3% in 2024, a 1 percentage point increase from 2023. It also made progress on the contribution to adjusted operating profits. The number remains small at 2.1%, but it's still a vast improvement over the 0.1% share in 2023.

#5. Market multiples look alright

BTI's market multiples are less attractive than the last I checked, but there's still a Buy case for the stock. First, the downside. Its forward non-GAAP price-to-earnings (P/E) for 2025 is now at 8.8x, compared with 7.9x in December. It's also higher than the five-year average ratio of 8.2x now, indicating a potential for ~7% decline in stock price.

However, it still remains competitive compared with peers. While PM continues to trade at a heavy premium as usual, with a forward P/E of 21.2x, BTI is trading at lower multiples than even Altria (MO) and IMBBY, which have forward P/Es of 10.6x and 9.1x respectively. Even if PM is removed from the equation, if BTI were to trade at the average of MO and IMBBY's P/Es, there would still be ~12% upside to the stock. In other words, there's more upside to the stock than not.

What next?

If aspects like recessionary conditions, a flight towards safer stocks and its healthy dividend yield are added to the mix, BTI could have an even bigger upside right now.

The company's fundamentals further add to its appeal. The growth in its new categories business is encouraging, especially as it expands its operating profits, which are still small but headed in the right direction. Further, despite a hit to its profit from provisions for legal settlements, the company's numbers till look healthy.

The earnings projections too, look alright and suggest that dividend levels can be sustained, if not increased even as the payout ratio is somewhat higher than comfortable right now. The earnings forecast also lends well to the market multiples, which are competitive compared with BTI's industry peers.

In sum, despite the price increase, the Buy case for the stock has only become stronger. The only caveat here is that if macroeconomic conditions quickly become stable again, there can be a circulation out of defensives once again, resulting in a drop in BTI's share price. However, even that will be a reason to buy more, because fundamentally nothing changes for it even then.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: The Turning Point Is For Real, Hang Tight
data.description:
Just when I thought the worst (regulatory and listing compliance issues) could be over for Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI), the market has had other ideas. Yet, simply blaming the market would have been truly unfair, as investors, analysts, strategists, and even the most prominent Wall Street CEOs were not just taken aback, but arguably left flummoxed by President Trump’s Liberation Day tariffs.

Given the “excitement” (read volatility) that his tariffs have already imposed on the market, I don’t think I need to remind you that the Nasdaq (QQQ, NDX) has already veered into a bear market, while the S&P 500 (SPX, SP500) also dropped to a bear market low this week, briefly joining the tech-heavy Nasdaq constituents.

With Trump’s tariffs practically freezing trade flows between the U.S. and China for now, what should we expect for Supermicro in the next phase of the trade war, even as negotiations between the U.S. and its trading partners (sans China) are ongoing at an accelerated pace.

Thankfully for the Charles Liang-led company, SMCI is assessed to be less exposed to Asia’s supply chain as compared to its other Taiwan-based ODMs peers. Hence, with its primarily domestic and USMCA-compliant manufacturing capacity, I think investors shouldn’t underrate SMCI’s propensity to capitalize on these localized advantages to capture market share gains if the tariffs uncertainties are expected to linger, with the 90-day deadline ticking down, as I pen this update.

While Taiwan is expected to provide substantial concessions to the U.S. in return for more favorable deals with President Trump, it remains to be seen what types of trade-offs could entail such a move, particularly on the more unwieldy and cumbersome non-tariff barriers, potentially hampering a speedy resolution.

In other words, Supermicro should offer investors a “clearer” play into the AI server rack supply chain, as it continues to benefit from robust hyperscale-led growth prospects, although there may have been some unanticipated hiccups in recent days based on adjustments on Microsoft's (MSFT) data center footprint. Despite that, Alphabet/Google (GOOGL) reassured the market that it has not materially revised its $75B CapEx plans for the year, while Microsoft’s adjustments could also have been taken out of context, possibly lowering the negative sentiments surrounding SMCI.

Hence, the competitive dynamics should heat up between Supermicro and its American peers such as Dell (DELL) and Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE), although the AI hardware peers have jointly suffered the de-rating by the market, even though SMCI continues to trade at a relative discount to sector peers. I believe the market is likely still shaken by the Trump administration’s resolve to institute a global reordering of trade to decouple American manufacturing from China beyond just selected industries that are pivotal to safeguard America’s national security (such as AI and semis).

Hence, the potential in investors being “wrong-footed” again with negative surprises by Trump 2.0 cannot be downplayed, particularly as the current exemptions for semiconductor related products might be dropped if Trump is insistent on bring back a much greater percentage of the AI value chain and situate them in America.

These could have result in markedly higher costs across the value chain, and possibly affecting SMCI’s already stressed working capital as it seeks to expand production, while crimping margins further as it ramps up Blackwell. Therefore, the implications of more intense competition and market share grab amid the current challenges could invite closer scrutiny in the next few months.

Wall Street analysts have already downgraded their outlook for Supermicro, but that’s not all to the story. The free cash flow margins could face substantive hurdles to return to profitability over the next year, as the trade war plays out.

Given the uncertainties after the 90-day deadline (extension or implementation), and the ambiguities relating to the semiconductor tariffs impact, we cannot really model these accurately right now.

Moreover, the threat of further spending pullbacks by the cloud computing players must also be contemplated, and the culmination of these headwinds are expected to take a material toll on Supermicro’s operating performance, even as it ramps up its Blackwell servers.

I believe even management might have difficulties in promulgating clarity through the rest of the first half, consistent with the recent earnings guidance observed in companies that posted their Q1 scorecard.

With that in mind, I believe we can move on to assessing whether the market has remained confident in SMCI’s bottoming thesis, even as a sustained recovery might take some time to pan out.

Based on my analysis, it seems like dip-buyers have made concerted efforts to underpin the $25 level, which also attracted robust buying support in February this year. Hence, the selling intensity appears to have stalled at a similar level this month, although the current undertakings between the U.S. and its trading partners in their trade negotiations could spur another bout of market volatility.

Despite that, it should be noted that SMCI’s forward PEG ratio of 0.35 is more than 70% below its tech sector peers. In addition, even its forward P/E of 13x is almost 20% under its five-year average. In other words, it seems like the stock has already been priced for steep pessimism, suggesting unanticipated upside or pleasant surprises (quick resolution of the trade conflict) might propel further buying momentum to return to SMCI, bolstering its current recovery further.

Investors could be wise to avoid buying into recovery surges closer to the $65 level, which met with stiff resistance in February. However, we are significantly below that level, suggesting the risk/reward seems relatively appealing, while having contemplated the valuations and also the growth prospects of Supermicro amid the current tariff challenges.

Rating: Maintain Buy.
